title: Fermented Cucumbers: Fermenting Sliced Cucumbers Two Ways
description: Learn how to ferment crunchy and crisp sliced cucumbers that stay delicious for weeks. This fermented cucumber recipe covers two slicing techniques and provides detailed fermentation guidance for perfect pickles every time.
steps: Ensure you are using the correct type of cucumber such as English cucumbers, pickling cucumbers, or Persian cocktail cucumbers, not slicing cucumbers which will become soggy.
Wash all fermentation equipment including the jar, weight, and lid in cool water.
Wash the cucumbers thoroughly in cool water.
Slice the cucumbers either as ripple cut chips or as spears and halves, making sure to remove both ends.
Place a kitchen scale on the counter, turn it on, and set it to measure in grams.
Weigh out all the designated ingredients according to the recipe amounts.
Add all ingredients to the mason jar, placing smaller cut items like shallots below the cucumbers to prevent floating.
If using dry spices, place them in cheesecloth or a tea bag and tie it closed, then nestle it at the bottom of the jar under the cucumbers.
Secure the lid on the jar and shake vigorously for 2 minutes to dissolve all the salt.
Remove the lid and place a clean fermentation weight in the jar, ensuring all cucumber pieces and the weight are fully submerged in the liquid.
Secure the lid back onto the jar without tightening it significantly.
Let the cucumbers ferment for 14 days at room temperature, placing the jar in a glass dish to catch any spills.
If the lid is tightened, burp the jar daily when it is actively bubbling to release excess pressure.
After 2 weeks, remove the fermentation weight and perform a smell and taste test to ensure the pickles smell pleasantly sour and taste tart and salty.
Store the finished fermented pickles in the refrigerator.
